rulings.Case Title: ALEXA RUDIN et al VS. HILL & COMPANY REAL ESTATE et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Ntc Of Mtn To Compel Further Responses To Fetter'S Special Interrogatories To Alexa Rudin And Ethan Rudin; Req. For Monetary Sanctions And Req. For Judicial Ntc
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing Thursday Feb. 16, 2012 Line 1 Part II - DEFENDANT ELIZABETH FETTER's Motion To Compel Further Responses To Fetter'S Special Interrogatories To Alexa Rudin And Ethan Rudin; Req. For Monetary Sanctions And Req. For Judicial notice. Therefore, within 15 calendar days of the entry of this order, Plaintiffs shall serve further responses, without objection, to Defendant's special interrogatories nos. 47, 51, 71, 75, 79, 98, 102, 106, 122, 138, 185, 186, 187, 188, 189, 190, 191, 192, 193, 194, 195, 196, 231, 239, 240, 241, 242, 243, 244, 245, 246, 247, 248, 249, 250, 251, 252, 253, 254, 262, 263, 271, 279, 280, 281, 282, 283, 284, 285, 286, 287, 288, 289, 290, 291, 292, 302, 303, 304, 305, 306, 311, 322, 323, 328, 330, and 331, which were originally propounded on July 28, 2011. However, Defendant's request for monetary sanctions is DENIED. The Court agrees that Plaintiffs misused the discovery process by stipulating that they would provide further responses and then refusing to comply with that agreement. However, the Court does take note of the fact that this Defendant alone has propounded nearly 1,000 special interrogatories to Plaintiffs in this action, the most recent of which came after both Plaintiffs had already been required to submit numerous days of deposition over the course of about a year and a half. Although the various forms of discovery generally may be employed in whatever order the parties see fit, see Code of Civil Procedure section 2019.020(a), it would seem that propounding such voluminous written discovery requests after extensive depositions had been conducted is out of proportion to the substance of, or need for, the information being sought through the interrogatories. And while Plaintiffs' explanation that Defendant's initial meet-and-confer letter apparently "fell through the cracks" is not itself sufficient to justify Plaintiffs' noncompliance, Defendant's counsel was aware, or should have been aware, that the particular counsel to whom the letter was addressed would still be recuperating from a medical emergency and thus not available to respond immediately. Defendant's counsel knew that another member of Plaintiffs' counsel was dealing with discovery matters in the meantime, yet directed that particularly significant piece of correspondence to the absent attorney. In light of these circumstances, therefore, the Court believes that an award of sanctions in this instance would be unjust. In the event that Plaintiffs fail to comply with this order, however, Defendant remains free to request monetary or other sanctions in a subsequent motion if appropriate. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to appeals@jimweixel.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: ALEXA RUDIN et al VS. HILL & COMPANY REAL ESTATE et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Ntc Of Mtn To Compel Further Responses To Fetter'S Special Interrogatories To Alexa Rudin And Ethan Rudin; Req. For Monetary Sanctions And Req. For Judicial Ntc
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday February 16, 2012 Line 1 - DEFENDANT ELIZABETH FETTER's Motion To Compel Further Responses To Fetter'S Special Interrogatories To Alexa Rudin And Ethan Rudin; Req. For Monetary Sanctions And Req. For Judicial Notice. Pro Tem Judge Jim Weixel, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Department 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge.The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Defendant's request for judicial notice of the Stipulation and Order to Continue Trial Date, filed on November 4, 2011, is GRANTED. Defendant's motion to compel further responses to Defendant's special interrogatories is GRANTED. Whatever arguable merit there might have been to Plaintiffs' objections to the interrogatories in question, the fact is that Plaintiffs agreed to provide further responses to Defendant's special interrogatories by December 5, 2011. That agreement formed a major basis for the parties' stipulation for a continuance of the trial date to April 30, 2012 and was incorporated within that stipulation and order.Defendant would be prejudiced if Plaintiffs were allowed to go back on that agreement now, especially with such a relatively short amount of time remaining until the trial date, and in light of the fact that discovery is now closed with the exception of the matters set forth in the stipulation and order.

rulings.Case Title: THE PAYROLL RESOURCE GROUP, A CALIFORNIA VS. ELIZABETH ECKE et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Ntc Of Motion To Compel Further Discovery Responses And Motion For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday Feb. 16, 2012 Line 2 - DEFENDANT ELIZABETH ECKE, PAYROLL SYSTEMS LLC.'s Motion To Compel Further Discovery Responses And Motion For Sanctions. Pro Tem Judge Jim Weixel, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Department 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge.The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Defendants' motion to compel further responses is GRANTED, with the exception of the matters stated below. Plaintiff's responses to the interrogatories and requests for production were evasive and incomplete, and the supplemental responses served by Plaintiff on January 30, 2012 did not correct those deficiencies.Furthermore, Plaintiff's objections based upon the trade secrets privilege were not substantiated. The Court's consideration of this point must proceed as follows:[T]he party claiming the [trade secrets] privilege has the burden of establishing its existence. [Citations.] Thereafter, the party seeking discovery must make a prima facie, particularized showing that the information sought is relevant and necessary to the proof of, or defense against, a material element of one or more causes of action presented in the case, and that it is reasonable to conclude that the information sought is essential to a fair resolution of the lawsuit. It is then up to the holder of the privilege to demonstrate any claimed disadvantages of a protective order. Either party may propose or oppose less intrusive alternatives to disclosure of the trade secret, but the burden is upon the trade secret claimant to demonstrate that an alternative to disclosure will not be unduly burdensome to the opposing side and that it will maintain the same fair balance in the litigation that would have been achieved by disclosure. Bridgestone/Firestone, Inc. v. Superior Court, 7 Cal.App.4th 1384, 1393 (1992). Assuming arguendo that the information and documents objected to by Plaintiff are subject to the privilege, it is plain that Plaintiff's claims - and Defendants' defenses - depend almost entirely upon the information and documents sought. Plaintiff has based its entire case upon the theory that Defendants misappropriated trade secrets and proprietary information in the form of client contact information and used that information to take clients from Plaintiff.

rulings.Case Title: THE PAYROLL RESOURCE GROUP, A CALIFORNIA VS. ELIZABETH ECKE et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendants Elizabeth Ecke, Payroll Systems Llc'S Motion To Compel Further Discovery Responses And Motion For Sanctions.
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday Feb. 16, 2012 Line 2 Part II - DEFENDANT ELIZABETH ECKE, PAYROLL SYSTEMS LLC.'s Motion To Compel Further Discovery Responses And Motion For Sanctions. Obviously, Defendants cannot respond to Plaintiff's allegations without knowing what information or materials Defendants are accused of misappropriating. The identification and production of such information and materials is essential not only to Defendants' ability to defend this action, but to Plaintiff's ability to prosecute it. Defendants cannot be expected to defend against Plaintiff's claims not only blindfolded, but in a vacuum as well. Plaintiff thus cannot show that any alternative to disclosure would not be unduly burdensome to Defendants and would maintain a fair balance that would have been achieved if the matters and documents were to be disclosed. Therefore, Defendants have overcome the trade secrets privilege's bar to disclosure.However, the Court agrees that at least some of the information and documents requested are potentially deserving of protection. Accordingly, within seven calendar days after the entry of this order, Plaintiff shall prepare and submit to Defendants a proposed protective order which addresses what materials and documents should be protected and how such protection should be afforded. (The parties are directed to the website of the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of California for suggested forms of protective orders; however, those forms are merely a suggestion and the parties are not required to use those forms.) Defendants shall have five calendar days after receipt of the proposed protective order in which to propose any changes to Plaintiff. If the parties agree on the form of the protective order within five calendar days after that point, Plaintiff shall submit the stipulated protective order to the Court for signature and entry without hearing. If the parties cannot agree on the form of the protective order within that time, the parties (or either of them) shall notify the Court accordingly, and the Court will enter a protective order in the form determined by the Court. Within 20 calendar days from the entry of the protective order, Plaintiff shall then serve further responses, without objection but subject to the protective order, to Defendants' Form Interrogatories Nos. 8.7, 9.1, 9.2, 17.1, and 50.1; Defendants' Special Interrogatories Nos. 13, 15, 16, 17, 22, 23, 25, 26, 36, and 37; and Defendants' Requests for Production Nos. 4, 5, 9, 28, 29, 32, 34, 41, 42, 44, 45, and 46, and shall produce all documents responsive to those requests. Any documents produced by Plaintiff shall be produced in the manner in which they were kept, or organized and labeled to correspond with the categories in Defendants' request for documents, as required by Code of Civil Procedure section 2031.280(a). If the documents are produced in a disorganized state due to the fact that Plaintiff kept the documents in such a state, Plaintiff shall produce a declaration or other verified response stating, upon personal knowledge, that such is the case. See Kayne v. Grande Holdings Ltd., 198 Cal.App.4th 1470, 1475-76 (2011). The Court finds, however, that Plaintiff should not be required to provide further responses to Defendants' Special Interrogatories Nos. 33 and 34 or to Defendants' Requests for Production Nos. 37 and 38. The requests for information and documents regarding each complaint received by Plaintiff, or any customer who terminated the relationship with Plaintiff, as to any customer from 2007 to the present

rulings.Case Title: THE PAYROLL RESOURCE GROUP, A CALIFORNIA VS. ELIZABETH ECKE et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendants Elizabeth Ecke, Payroll Systems Llc'S Motion To Compel Further Discovery Responses And Motion For Sanctions.
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday Feb. 16, 2012 Line 2 Part IV - DEFENDANT ELIZABETH ECKE, PAYROLL SYSTEMS LLC.'s Motion To Compel Further Discovery Responses And Motion For Sanctions. Defendants' counsel states in his declaration that he has expended a total of 19 hours at $250 per hour, and his colleague has expended 2.3 hours at a rate of $365 per hour, to prepare the moving papers, review Defendants' opposition, prepare a reply, and appear at hearing. The Court believes that a total of 14 hours and two hours, respectively, are more in line with the effort required in bringing this motion and obtaining this order. Applying counsel's hourly rates, which the Court finds to be reasonable, this arrives at a total of $4,230.00 (14 x $250 = $3,500.00, plus 2.0 x $365 = $730.00). Adding the $40 motion fee, that brings the attorney fees and costs incurred by Defendants to $4,270.00. Accordingly, Plaintiff shall pay monetary sanctions of $4,270.00 to Defendants within 30 days of the entry of this order. The Court declines to impose any other form of sanctions at this time since this is the first order entered as to these discovery disputes. See Code Civ. Proc. §§ 2030.300(e), 2031.310(i) (contemplating that issue sanctions, terminating sanctions, and other non-monetary sanctions will be imposed only following a responding party's noncompliance with previous order compelling further responses).Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to appeals@jimweixel.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: THE PAYROLL RESOURCE GROUP, A CALIFORNIA VS. ELIZABETH ECKE et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendants Elizabeth Ecke, Payroll Systems Llc'S Motion To Compel Further Discovery Responses And Motion For Sanctions.
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday Feb. 16, 2012 Line 2 Part III - DEFENDANT ELIZABETH ECKE, PAYROLL SYSTEMS LLC.'s Motion To Compel Further Discovery Responses And Motion For Sanctions. regardless of the reason for the dissatisfaction, the scope of the complaint, or the reason for the termination of the customer's relationship with Plaintiff - are overbroad and not reasonably calculated to lead to the discovery of admissible evidence. Therefore, Defendants' motion is DENIED as to those special interrogatories and requests for production. Defendants' motion for sanctions against Plaintiff is GRANTED for monetary relief only. While in some circumstances the service of supplemental responses to discovery following the filing of a motion to compel can obviate the need for the Court to determine the motion and can allow a party to avoid sanctions, in this case no such considerations exist. As Defendants point out in their reply papers, Plaintiff failed to respond to Defendants' meet and confer efforts, and did not serve supplemental responses to Defendants' interrogatories (but not the requests for production) until well after the motion to compel had been prepared and filed. And although Plaintiff suggested that the parties agree to a protective order with respect to matters claimed to be subject to the trade secret privilege, that suggestion was not made until well after the motion had been made, and even then Plaintiff did not provide a proposed form for such a protective order. These tactics prejudiced Defendants by forcing them to file the motion on the last available day and incur expenses in doing so, when many of Plaintiff's objections could have been addressed far earlier. Accordingly, the Court finds that Plaintiff has engaged in misuse of the discovery process, and that Plaintiff's opposition to the motion was without substantial justification and that no other circumstances make the imposition of sanctions unjust.

rulings.Case Title: UNION PACIFIC RAILROAD COMPANY, VS. UNIVERSAL PARAGON CORPORATION, et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, February 16, 2012, Line 13, PLAINTIFF UNION PACIFIC RAILROAD COMPANY, MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT - The motion for summary judgment is granted. The Indemnity Agreements at issue include a covenant for indemnity against loss and a covenant for indemnity against liability. Broadly construed, Plaintiff's Amended Complaint seeks recovery under both covenants. (Amended Complaint paragraphs 9, 24, 29-30). The statute of limitations on Plaintiff's causes of action accrued when Plaintiff paid the loss or when liability was incurred. The later of the two statute of limitations controls. (Globe Indemnity Company v. Larkin (1944) 62 Cal.App.2d 891, 893), The statute of limitations on the covenant for indemnity against liability began to run on March 15, 2011, the date the liability determination was made in the underlying action.. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and in compliance with Code of Civil Procedure 437c(g) and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/HK

rulings.Case Title: NILS LOMMERIN VS. SHERYL ANDERSON, AN INDIVIDUAL DOING BUSINESS AS et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff Nils Lommerin'S Motion To Compel Further Responses To Special Intererogatrires, Form Interrgoatories
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday Feb. 16, 2012 Line 3 Part III - Plaintiff Nils Lommerin'S Motion To Compel Further Responses To Special Intererogatrires, Form Interrgoatories. There was no reason why the protective order could not have been entered, by one party or the other, well before it actually was. Doing so probably might have avoided - or at least deferred - most of the disputes here and, eventually, the necessity of this motion. Instead of following up with each other about how the protective order should be entered and when further responses and production of documents should be made, the parties simply continued to direct demands for action to each other without any attempt to truly agree on who should do what, and when it should be done.However, the fact does remain that Defendant only produced documents to Plaintiff well after the protective order was entered, and apparently waited until the week before this hearing (at which point all briefing had closed) in which to do so. This is not sufficient to avoid the imposition of some measure of sanctions, since Plaintiff had already incurred substantial expense and gone to significant efforts to secure compliance and bring this motion. While the Court finds that Plaintiff's motion should be granted and that some measure of monetary sanctions are in order, the Court believes that the amount of sanctions awarded should be reduced by 25% because many of the issues decided in this motion could have been avoided with better meet-and-confer efforts and communication between counsel. Accordingly, Defendant is ordered to pay monetary sanctions to Plaintiff in the amount of $3,405.00 within 30 days of the entry of this motion.Defendant's request for sanctions against Plaintiff and his counsel is DENIED.Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to appeals@jimweixel.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: NILS LOMMERIN VS. SHERYL ANDERSON, AN INDIVIDUAL DOING BUSINESS AS et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff Nils Lommerin'S Motion To Compel Further Responses To Special Intererogatrires, Form Interrgoatories
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday Feb 16, 2012 Line 3 Part II - Plaintiff Nils Lommerin'S Motion To Compel Further Responses To Special Intererogatrires, Form Interrgoatories. Plaintiff's motion to compel is GRANTED as to Plaintiff's special interrogatories nos. 1, 2, 3, 8, 9, 10, 11, 13, 15, 16, 18, 19, 21, 22, 23, 25, 26, and 34; form interrogatories nos. 9.1, 9.2, and 17.1; and requests for production of documents nos. 1, 3, 5, 6, 9, 12, 13, 14, 15, and 16. Defendant shall serve further responses to said interrogatories and requests, along with all responsive documents not already produced, no later than 20 calendar days from the entry of this order. Said responses and production of documents shall be made without further objection but subject to the protective order of January 24, 2012. If Defendant believes that she is entitled to produce documents in lieu of a narrative or summary response to interrogatories in accordance with Code of Civil Procedure section 2030.230, she shall produce said documents in groups labeled according to the particular interrogatory as to which the documents are being produced in response. It is not sufficient for Defendant to simply produce a mass of documents with a response that the documents responsive to a particular interrogatory may be found somewhere within the universe of documents produced, or within a vague and undefined category of documents. See Code Civ. Proc. § 2030.230 (requiring responding party to "specify the writings from which the answer may be derived or ascertained¿ in sufficient detail to permit the propounding party to locate and to identify, as readily as the responding party can, the documents from which the answer may be ascertained"). This directive shall apply to any documents already produced as well as documents to be produced following this order. Plaintiff's objection to the Declaration of Randy Sullivan is OVERRULED. Although the original declaration was not signed by Mr. Sullivan, but by his colleague, Caitlin Connell, Esq., Mr. Sullivan has provided a version with his signature. The Court regards this as an adoption by Mr. Sullivan of the statements in the declaration and a sufficient remedy of any previous deficiency in the declaration.Plaintiff's request for sanctions is GRANTED in the amount of $3,405.00. Many of the parties' complaints about each other's discovery conduct stem from the fact that neither party seemed to want to take the initiative to secure the entry of the protective order that had been agreed upon by the parties. Both sides appeared to be under the impression that a formal ex parte appearance was required for the protective order to be entered, when in fact the order could have been entered through the simple mechanism of a stipulation without a hearing.

rulings.Case Title: NILS LOMMERIN VS. SHERYL ANDERSON, AN INDIVIDUAL DOING BUSINESS AS et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Noticeof Motion And Motion To Compel Further Responses To Special Intererogatrires, Form Interrgoatories
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday Feb. 16, 2012 Line 3 - PLAINTIFF NILS LOMMERIN's Motion And Motion To Compel Further Responses To Special Intererogatrires, Form Interrgoatories. Pro Tem Judge Jim Weixel, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Department 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The Court wishes to disclose that this temporary judge, while in private practice in Marin County between 2002 and 2005, was counsel of record in an action in Contra Costa Superior Court in which Spencer Martinez, Esq., counsel for the moving party herein, was one of the opposing counsel on the matter. This temporary judge has also met Mr. Martinez on a few occasions in chance meetings at professional or social events after that time. This temporary judge knows of no time since 2005 at which this temporary judge or Mr. Martinez been involved in the same case in any capacity, nor of any circumstance that might indicate the presence of a conflict or bias for or against any party in this action, or cause the Court's impartiality to be reasonably questioned. However, the Court wishes to disclose these facts out of an abundance of caution. The Court is in receipt of Defendant's "sur-reply" filed on February 14, 2012. Although such a response is not provided for by statute or rule, the Court notes that Defendant asserts therein that she has produced documents to Plaintiff on February 7, 2012. Accordingly, the Court will take note of the fact that Defendant has represented that said documents have been produced.

rulings.Case Title: SHANNON CRAIG JACKSON et al VS. ADRIENNE BROWN-EDINGTON et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO CONSOLIDATE ACTIONS OF CASE(S) PTR-11-294889 WITH CASE CGC-11-513313

The two cases are consolidated for all purposes with PTR-11-294889 being the lead case and henceforth all papers filed only under that case number. All matters in both cases will be heard in the probate division. The February 29 hearing in the probate division is confirmed. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. Plaintiffs are required to prepare proposed orders with the captions of both cases repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed orders to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/HK
